content.text: The flow-matching objective is a training criterion for continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) that enables learning a time-dependent vector field v_theta(t, x) which transports samples from a simple source distribution p0 (typically a standard Gaussian) to a complex target distribution p1 (the data distribution) by solving an ordinary differential equation (ODE). Unlike diffusion models that require simulating a stochastic differential equation (SDE) with many noisy steps, flow matching learns a deterministic vector field and generates samples by integrating that field from t=0 to t=1 using any standard ODE solver.

The key mathematical insight is that the marginal probability path p_t connecting p0 and p1 defines an underlying vector field u_t(x) — the unique vector field whose associated ODE transports probability mass along the path. Flow matching trains a neural network to regress this vector field directly: L_FM = E[||v_theta(t, x) - u_t(x)||^2]. The practical breakthrough of Lipman et al. (2022) and Liu et al. (2022) was showing that this objective can be computed efficiently using conditional flows rather than marginal flows, removing the need for expensive maximum likelihood estimation via ODE solving during training.

Flow matching generalizes and unifies several generative modeling approaches. When the conditional paths are linear interpolations between noise and data with Gaussian perturbations, flow matching recovers the DDPM training objective. When optimal transport is used to define the conditional paths, flow matching produces straighter trajectories that require fewer NFE (number of function evaluations) at inference time. This makes flow matching the foundation of state-of-the-art image and video generation systems including Stable Diffusion 3 and CogVideoX.

Diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art generation quality but have two practical limitations: (1) they require many sampling steps (25-1000 DDPM steps or 20-100 DDIM steps) to produce high-quality samples, driving up inference latency and cost; and (2) their training objective (score matching) requires careful noise schedule design and is deeply coupled to the Gaussian perturbation kernel. Flow matching addresses both limitations.

By learning a deterministic ODE vector field rather than a stochastic score function, flow matching produces straighter sample trajectories. Straighter trajectories can be accurately integrated with fewer steps. Optimal transport flow matching (OT-CFM) produces near-linear paths from noise to data; in practice, high-quality image samples are achievable in 4-8 NFE with flow matching versus 20+ with DDIM. At scale (billions of parameters, millions of images), this inference efficiency difference translates to significant serving cost reduction.

Flow matching is also more architecturally flexible: the vector field can be parameterized by any architecture (Transformer, CNN, UNet, DiT), and the ODE integration is agnostic to the parameterization. This has enabled the flow matching training objective to be applied to DiT (Diffusion Transformer) architectures, producing the current generation of high-resolution image and video models.

attrs.alt: Flow matching: neural network learns vector field that transports probability mass from Gaussian source p0 to data target p1 along learned paths

content.text: OT-CFM produces near-linear trajectories; high-quality samples achievable in 4-8 NFE versus 20-100 for diffusion models.
The conditional flow matching objective does not require ODE solving during training, unlike maximum likelihood CNF training which is O(d^2) or O(d^3) per step.
ODE integration is deterministic given an initial noise sample; enables reproducible generation and smooth interpolation in latent space.
Any interpolant between p0 and p1 can be used (linear, cosine, optimal transport); different interpolants give different trajectory shapes without changing the training procedure.
When Gaussian conditional paths are used, the flow matching objective is equivalent to the diffusion score matching objective, providing a unified theoretical framework.
Flow matching works cleanly with DiT architectures, enabling scaling laws to apply to flow-based generation.
The quality-NFE tradeoff depends heavily on the ODE solver (Euler, Heun, DPM-Solver, etc.). Naive Euler integration requires more steps than adaptive solvers.

Unlike DDPM which adds stochastic noise at each step (acting as a self-correcting mechanism for modeling errors), ODE integration is deterministic — accumulated errors compound without correction. Stochastic samplers (Langevin, SDE-based) can be layered on top but complicate the pipeline.

Flow matching with OT paths requires nearest-neighbor matching at each training batch to find optimal couplings, which is O(N^2) per batch. Minibatch OT approximations (e.g., random pairs) introduce bias.

The vector field prediction quality depends on the architecture conditioning on time t. Time conditioning must be expressive enough (e.g., sinusoidal time embeddings or adaptive layer norm) to represent the time-varying nature of the field.

The flow-matching objective (Lipman et al., 2022) is a simulation-free method for training continuous normalising flows that bypasses the expensive ODE solve required by FFJORD. The core insight is that instead of regressing the intractable marginal velocity field, we can regress conditional velocity fields — known in closed form for simple Gaussian conditional paths — and this conditional objective has the same unique minimiser as the marginal one. This makes training as cheap as standard regression while retaining the generative power of CNFs.
Marginal Flow Matching: The Intractable Ideal

Given a source distribution p₀ (e.g. Gaussian) and a target data distribution q, we want to learn a velocity field v_θ whose probability-flow ODE transports p₀ to q. The marginal flow matching objective regresses the true marginal velocity u_t(x) — the velocity that generates the marginal pₜ:

The problem: u_t(x) is the aggregated velocity from all data points x₁ flowing through position x at time t — it requires marginalising over all data points at once. With an implicit marginal distribution, neither pₜ nor u_t(x) is tractable to compute.
Conditional Flow Matching: The Tractable Reformulation
For each data point x₁, define a conditional probability path pₜ(·|x₁) and the conditional velocity u_t(x|x₁) that generates it. The conditional flow matching (CFM) objective is:

Key theorem (Lipman et al.): L_FM and L_CFM have the same gradient w.r.t. θ, and therefore the same minimiser. This means training with the cheap, tractable CFM objective is equivalent to training with the intractable marginal FM objective.
The proof uses the fact that the marginal velocity is the conditional expectation: u_t(x) = E[u_t(x|x₁) | x ~ pₜ(x)], so the two objectives differ only by a term independent of θ.
The simplest choice is a Gaussian conditional path that interpolates between noise and data:
with affine mean and standard deviation: μₜ(x₁) = αₜ·x₁ and σₜ(x₁) = σₜ (a scalar schedule). A sample from pₜ(·|x₁) is then:
The conditional velocity is the time-derivative of the mean trajectory holding ε fixed:
This is fully closed-form. Different schedule choices recover known methods:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


def cfm_sample_and_target(
    x0: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D) noise
    x1: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D) data
    t:  torch.Tensor,    # (B,) time in [0,1]
    path: str = "linear",
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    Returns (x_t, target_velocity) for the conditional flow matching loss.
    Works for any path choice.
    """
    B = x1.size(0)
    t4 = t.view(B, *([1] * (x1.dim() - 1)))   # broadcast shape

    if path == "linear":
        # alpha_t = t, sigma_t = 1 - t
        x_t = (1 - t4) * x0 + t4 * x1
        velocity = x1 - x0   # constant along trajectory

    elif path == "cosine":
        # alpha_t = sin(pi*t/2), sigma_t = cos(pi*t/2)
        alpha = torch.sin(math.pi / 2 * t4)
        sigma = torch.cos(math.pi / 2 * t4)
        d_alpha = math.pi / 2 * torch.cos(math.pi / 2 * t4)
        d_sigma = -math.pi / 2 * torch.sin(math.pi / 2 * t4)
        x_t     = alpha * x1 + sigma * x0
        velocity = d_alpha * x1 + d_sigma * x0

    elif path == "vp":
        # Linear-in-log-SNR schedule as in DDPM
        beta_min, beta_max = 0.1, 20.0
        # Integral of beta from 0 to t
        log_snr_t = -0.5 * (beta_min * t + 0.5 * (beta_max - beta_min) * t ** 2)
        alpha  = torch.exp(0.5 * log_snr_t).view(B, *([1] * (x1.dim() - 1)))
        sigma  = torch.sqrt(1 - alpha ** 2)
        d_alpha = alpha * (-0.5 * (beta_min + (beta_max - beta_min) * t4))
        d_sigma = -alpha * d_alpha / sigma
        x_t     = alpha * x1 + sigma * x0
        velocity = d_alpha * x1 + d_sigma * x0

    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown path: {path}")

    return x_t, velocity


def cfm_loss(
    model: nn.Module,
    x1: torch.Tensor,
    path: str = "linear",
) -> torch.Tensor:
    B = x1.size(0)
    x0 = torch.randn_like(x1)
    t  = torch.rand(B, device=x1.device)
    x_t, target_v = cfm_sample_and_target(x0, x1, t, path=path)
    pred_v = model(x_t, t)
    return F.mse_loss(pred_v, target_v)

Standard CFM pairs each noise sample x₀ with a data sample x₁ uniformly at random. This random coupling creates many crossing trajectories — pairs where one trajectory must travel a longer path than necessary. OT-CFM (Tong et al., 2023) instead uses a mini-batch optimal transport coupling that minimises average transport cost between x₀ and x₁ within each batch:

With OT coupling, conditional paths between matched pairs tend not to cross — the trained velocity field is straighter, enabling fewer ODE steps at inference. OT-CFM recovers straight-line paths similar to Rectified Flow but without requiring the iterative reflow procedure.

def minibatch_ot_coupling(
    x0: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D)
    x1: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D)
) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    Compute mini-batch OT coupling via the Hungarian algorithm (exact for B < ~1000).
    Returns reordered (x0_ot, x1) where x0_ot[i] is the optimal x0 matched to x1[i].
    """
    from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment
    import numpy as np

    B = x0.size(0)
    # Cost matrix: squared L2 distances
    cost = torch.cdist(x0, x1, p=2).pow(2)   # (B, B)
    cost_np = cost.detach().cpu().numpy()

    # Hungarian assignment: minimise total cost
    row_idx, col_idx = linear_sum_assignment(cost_np)
    # col_idx[i] gives which x1 sample is matched to x0[i]
    # We want to reorder x0 so that x0_ot[i] matches x1[i]
    x0_ot = x0[row_idx][col_idx.argsort()]
    return x0_ot, x1


def ot_cfm_loss(
    model: nn.Module,
    x1: torch.Tensor,    # (B, D) data samples
    path: str = "linear",
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """OT-CFM: use mini-batch OT coupling before computing CFM loss."""
    x0 = torch.randn_like(x1)
    x0_ot, x1 = minibatch_ot_coupling(x0, x1)
    t  = torch.rand(x1.size(0), device=x1.device)
    x_t, target_v = cfm_sample_and_target(x0_ot, x1, t, path=path)
    return F.mse_loss(model(x_t, t), target_v)
Connection to Score Matching and Diffusion
Flow matching and diffusion score matching train the same class of models with equivalent objectives under the VP-SDE schedule. The score function and velocity field are related by:

attrs.latex: v_t(x) = f(x,t) - \frac{1}{2}g(t)^2 s_\theta(x,t) \qquad \text{(PF-ODE velocity from score)}

content.text: For VP-SDE with linear schedule, regressing u_t(x|x₁) = ẋ₁·αₜ + ẋ₀·σₜ is equivalent to predicting the noise ε = x₀ as used in DDPM. Flow matching is therefore a generalisation that works for any probability path, not just the specific paths induced by diffusion SDEs — and it can handle paths with less curvature (e.g. linear) that diffusion models cannot represent.

Marginal flow matching asks you to learn the average direction all cars on a road are travelling at each point — but you can't observe all the cars, only their density. Conditional flow matching observes each car individually: you know where car i started (x₀) and where it's going (x₁), so you can compute its velocity exactly. The theorem says that a network trained to predict each individual car's velocity will learn to predict the average velocity correctly — the same function as the intractable marginal objective — because the individual targets aggregate to the same mean.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class SinusoidalTimeEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """Encode scalar time t in [0,1] to a sinusoidal embedding."""
    def __init__(self, dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.dim = dim

    def forward(self, t):
        # t: (batch,) scalar times
        half = self.dim // 2
        freqs = torch.exp(
            -torch.arange(half, device=t.device) * (torch.log(torch.tensor(10000.0)) / (half - 1))
        )
        args = t[:, None] * freqs[None, :]  # (batch, half)
        return torch.cat([torch.cos(args), torch.sin(args)], dim=-1)  # (batch, dim)


class VectorFieldNet(nn.Module):
    """Simple MLP vector field for 2D toy flow matching demo."""
    def __init__(self, data_dim=2, time_dim=64, hidden=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.time_emb = SinusoidalTimeEmbedding(time_dim)
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(data_dim + time_dim, hidden),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.SiLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, data_dim),
        )

    def forward(self, x, t):
        # x: (batch, data_dim), t: (batch,)
        t_emb = self.time_emb(t)
        return self.net(torch.cat([x, t_emb], dim=-1))


def conditional_flow_matching_loss(model, x1, sigma_min=1e-4):
    """
    Linear conditional flow matching loss (Lipman et al. 2022).

    Conditional path: x_t = (1 - (1 - sigma_min)*t) * x0 + t * x1
    Conditional vector field: u_t(x|x1) = x1 - (1 - sigma_min)*x0
    where x0 ~ N(0, I)
    """
    batch = x1.shape[0]
    device = x1.device

    # Sample t uniformly in [0, 1]
    t = torch.rand(batch, device=device)

    # Sample x0 ~ N(0, I) as source noise
    x0 = torch.randn_like(x1)

    # Interpolate: x_t = (1 - (1-sigma_min)*t) * x0 + t * x1
    t_expand = t.view(batch, *([1] * (x1.dim() - 1)))
    x_t = (1 - (1 - sigma_min) * t_expand) * x0 + t_expand * x1

    # Target vector field: u_t(x_t | x1) = x1 - (1 - sigma_min)*x0
    u_t = x1 - (1 - sigma_min) * x0

    # Predict vector field
    v_pred = model(x_t, t)

    # MSE regression loss
    return F.mse_loss(v_pred, u_t)


# Training loop
model = VectorFieldNet(data_dim=2)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)

# Dummy 2D data for illustration (replace with real samples from p1)
def sample_data(n):
    # Two-moon dataset or Gaussian mixture
    return torch.randn(n, 2) * 0.5 + torch.tensor([2.0, 0.0])

for step in range(5000):
    x1 = sample_data(256)
    loss = conditional_flow_matching_loss(model, x1)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    if step % 500 == 0:
        print(f"Step {step}, loss={loss.item():.4f}")


# Sampling: integrate ODE from t=0 to t=1 using Euler method
@torch.no_grad()
def sample_euler(model, n=100, steps=100):
    x = torch.randn(n, 2)  # x0 ~ p0 = N(0,I)
    dt = 1.0 / steps
    for i in range(steps):
        t = torch.full((n,), i * dt)
        v = model(x, t)
        x = x + v * dt
    return x

samples = sample_euler(model, n=500, steps=50)
print("Generated samples shape:", samples.shape)

OT-CFM (Tong et al. 2023) improves upon basic CFM by coupling noise samples x0 to data samples x1 using minibatch optimal transport, producing straighter trajectories that require fewer integration steps at inference:

import torch
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment

def minibatch_ot_coupling(x0, x1):
    """
    Compute approximate OT coupling using linear assignment on L2 cost.
    Returns reordered x0 matched to x1 for straighter paths.
    """
    # Cost matrix: pairwise L2 distances
    # x0, x1: (batch, dim)
    with torch.no_grad():
        diff = x0.unsqueeze(1) - x1.unsqueeze(0)  # (batch, batch, dim)
        cost = (diff ** 2).sum(-1).cpu().numpy()   # (batch, batch)
    row_ind, col_ind = linear_sum_assignment(cost)
    return x0[row_ind], x1[col_ind]


def ot_conditional_flow_matching_loss(model, x1, sigma_min=1e-4):
    """OT-CFM: pair x0 with x1 via minibatch optimal transport."""
    batch = x1.shape[0]
    device = x1.device
    t = torch.rand(batch, device=device)
    x0 = torch.randn_like(x1)

    # OT coupling
    x0_matched, x1_matched = minibatch_ot_coupling(x0.cpu(), x1.cpu())
    x0_matched = x0_matched.to(device)
    x1_matched = x1_matched.to(device)

    t_expand = t.view(batch, *([1] * (x1.dim() - 1)))
    x_t = (1 - (1 - sigma_min) * t_expand) * x0_matched + t_expand * x1_matched
    u_t = x1_matched - (1 - sigma_min) * x0_matched

    v_pred = model(x_t, t)
    return torch.nn.functional.mse_loss(v_pred, u_t)
Examples: Flow Matching for Images with DiT

# Conceptual sketch of how flow matching is applied in DiT-based image models
# (Simplified from Stable Diffusion 3 / FLUX architecture)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DiTBlock(nn.Module):
    """Single DiT block with adaptive layer norm conditioned on time + label."""
    def __init__(self, dim, heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(dim, elementwise_affine=False)
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(dim, heads, batch_first=True)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(dim, elementwise_affine=False)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(dim, 4*dim), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(4*dim, dim))
        # Adaptive layer norm scale+shift from conditioning
        self.adaLN = nn.Linear(dim, 6 * dim)

    def forward(self, x, c):
        # c: conditioning vector (time emb + label emb)
        shift1, scale1, gate1, shift2, scale2, gate2 = self.adaLN(c).chunk(6, dim=-1)
        # Apply adaptive layer norm
        x_norm = self.norm1(x) * (1 + scale1.unsqueeze(1)) + shift1.unsqueeze(1)
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(x_norm, x_norm, x_norm)
        x = x + gate1.unsqueeze(1) * attn_out
        x_norm = self.norm2(x) * (1 + scale2.unsqueeze(1)) + shift2.unsqueeze(1)
        x = x + gate2.unsqueeze(1) * self.ff(x_norm)
        return x

# Training: same CFM loss, x1 = patchified latent image, x_t = interpolated
# Sampling: Euler or DPM-Solver++ integration of the learned vector field
# 8-20 steps achieves high quality; 50 steps matches diffusion quality
print("DiT block for flow matching defined")
Using naive Euler with too few steps:
Euler integration with 4-8 steps introduces significant discretization error for non-linear trajectories. Use Heun (2nd order) or DPM-Solver++ for the same step count with much better quality.
Setting sigma_min=0 makes the conditional path collapse to a point at t=1 with zero variance. Always use a small sigma_min (1e-4 to 1e-3) to keep the conditional distribution well-defined.
Uniform time sampling during training undersamples the high-curvature regions near t=0 and t=1. Logit-normal time sampling improves training efficiency for complex data distributions.
The vector field must be a function of both x and t. Omitting time conditioning produces a time-invariant field that cannot represent the changing transport direction along the trajectory.
The same model produces very different sample quality with Euler (100 steps) versus DPM-Solver++ (20 steps). Always benchmark the NFE-quality tradeoff on your specific model and data.
Optimal transport coupling produces straighter paths and requires 2-4x fewer NFE for equivalent quality compared to independent coupling.
Sample t from logit-Normal(0,1) rather than Uniform(0,1) to focus training capacity on the high-curvature middle of the trajectory.
Use Heun or DPM-Solver++ at inference:
Second-order or higher solvers substantially improve quality-NFE tradeoff. Heun adds only one extra function evaluation per step but roughly halves the required number of steps.
Apply CFG by training with 10-20% unconditional dropout and sampling with guidance scale 2-7. The same CFG formulation used for diffusion models works identically for flow matching.
Validate with FID and CLIP score:
Track both FID (distribution quality) and CLIP score (text-image alignment) during training; they measure different failure modes of the learned vector field.

Flow matching scales along the same axes as diffusion models: model parameters, data volume, and compute. At 1B+ parameters with DiT architectures, flow matching achieves state-of-the-art FID on ImageNet-256. The key efficiency gain over diffusion is at inference time:

Training compute follows the same Chinchilla-like scaling laws as autoregressive and diffusion models. Flow matching does not provide training compute savings over diffusion models — the efficiency gains are purely at inference time through trajectory straightness.
PyTorch ODE solver library supporting Euler, Heun, Runge-Kutta, and adaptive solvers. The standard choice for research flow matching implementations.
For stochastic variants; provides SDE solvers needed when adding Langevin noise correction on top of flow matching.
Meta's open-source flow matching library with optimized CFM, OT-CFM, and exact likelihood computation.
Hugging Face diffusers supports flow-based schedulers (FlowMatchEulerDiscreteScheduler) for Stable Diffusion 3 and FLUX models.
Efficient OT computation for minibatch OT coupling; includes Sinkhorn algorithm for regularized OT.

Like diffusion models, flow matching models trained on private data can memorize and reproduce training examples. Apply differential privacy training or membership inference audits before deploying on sensitive corpora.

Adaptive ODE solvers (e.g., dopri5) can fail with NaN or step-size collapses if the learned vector field has large Lipschitz constants. Always cap max_norm of the vector field predictions and monitor for solver failures in production.
Very high classifier-free guidance scales (>10) can cause color saturation artifacts and mode collapse in image generation. Validate guidance scale on a held-out prompt set before deployment.

ODE integration is deterministic given the same initial noise and ODE solver; unlike SDE-based methods, the same seed always produces the same output. This is a reliability advantage for auditing and debugging.
Flow matching trains a neural network to regress a time-dependent vector field v_theta(t,x) that transports samples from Gaussian noise p0 to the data distribution p1 via an ODE.
The conditional flow matching (CFM) objective makes training simulation-free by regressing the conditional rather than the marginal vector field, enabling efficient training without ODE solving.
OT-CFM uses optimal transport to couple noise and data samples, producing straighter trajectories that achieve equivalent quality with 2-4x fewer NFE at inference time.
Flow matching generalizes diffusion models: Gaussian CFM with linear interpolants recovers the DDPM training objective as a special case.
At inference, quality depends heavily on the ODE solver; second-order methods like Heun or DPM-Solver++ achieve substantially better quality than Euler at the same NFE budget.
Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX, and CogVideoX use flow matching with DiT architectures, representing the current state of the art in high-resolution image and video generation.
Classifier-free guidance from diffusion models applies directly to flow matching and remains the primary mechanism for conditioning generation quality on text prompts.
Always use a small sigma_min (1e-4) in the conditional path to avoid degenerate distributions, and use logit-normal time sampling for better training efficiency.
